title: Combinatorial and Geometric Challenges in PAC Learning with Partial Concepts
speaker: Shay Moran
abstract:
I will describe a recent extension of the classical PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning framework [Vapnik and Chervonenkis, 1970s; Valiant, 1980s]. This extension makes it possible to model a wide range of common and practical data-dependent assumptions, such as margin conditions or low-dimensional structure. The key idea is to move from total function classes to partial function classes, where functions may be undefined on certain regions, reflecting assumptions about where the data lie. This perspective reveals phenomena fundamentally different from the classical PAC model. For example, the classical empirical risk minimization (ERM) principle — which fully characterizes learnability in the classical PAC framework and is viewed as the central algorithmic paradigm — fails for general partial function classes.
I will then turn to the broader question of how much more expressive partial functions are compared to traditional total functions. This leads to new and open problems at the interface of combinatorics, geometry, and topology, which I will present in the talk. The talk will not assume prior knowledge in learning theory.

proof_stages.label: PAC learning formalism and learning curves
proof_stages.summary: The talk formalizes binary supervised learning by distributions over examples, iid training samples, classifiers, test error, learning curves, realizability, and PAC learnability as a uniform vanishing error bound over all realizable distributions.

proof_stages.label: VC characterization and empirical-risk principle
proof_stages.summary: Classical PAC learnability is identified with finite VC dimension, with the lower bound coming from random labels on large shattered sets and the upper bound yielding uniform convergence and the consistent/empirical-risk-minimization learning principle.

proof_stages.label: Partial concepts as supported classifiers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces partial functions with undefined star-values to encode assumptions such as margins or restricted data supports, extends realizability and VC dimension by ignoring stars, and states that finite VC dimension still characterizes learnability though the classical uniform-convergence/ERM mechanism no longer suffices.

proof_stages.label: Disambiguation obstruction to reducing partial to total learning
proof_stages.summary: The impossibility of generally reducing partial concept learning to total concept learning is reformulated as a combinatorial disambiguation problem, and the speaker reports a VC-dimension-one partial class whose every total completion has unbounded VC dimension, obtained through graph-theoretic and communication-complexity phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Natural geometric partial classes
proof_stages.summary: The talk asks whether natural partial classes, especially large-dimensional halfspaces with margin and metric-space threshold concepts, admit bounded-complexity disambiguations, noting known VC bounds, dimension blow-ups under naive completions, and a sharp one-quarter margin threshold for one metric-space variant.

proof_stages.label: Spherical dimension via realizability complexes
proof_stages.summary: For finite concept classes the speaker builds the simplicial complex of realizable datasets, interprets VC dimension as the largest cross-polytope inside it, defines a topological spherical dimension by antipodal spheres, and relates bounding this dimension to disambiguating margin halfspaces.

proof_stages.label: Covering formulations and further learning-theoretic applications
proof_stages.summary: The topological obstruction is recast as low-VC bounded-diameter or antipodal covering questions for Euclidean spaces and spheres, then connected to lower bounds for replicable learning and for reductions from classification to optimization before the talk’s recap.

examples.name: All-star-or-one partial class
examples.note: In response to a question about why disambiguation is hard, the speaker uses the toy class with only stars and ones to show that a naive zero-filling can create an extremely complex total class even though another filling is easy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the partial class with only stars or ones, notes its VC dimension is zero, and checks how filling stars with zeros yields all subsets.

examples.name: Concentric balls without bounded diameter
examples.note: The chain of concentric balls gives a low-VC open cover when diameters are unbounded, thereby showing why the bounded-diameter hypothesis is needed in the covering question.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the unbounded concentric-ball cover and checks that combinatorially it forms a chain of VC dimension one.
